metadata.title: Why a Particle Bounces Off a Cliff It Could Easily Clear
metadata.note: Clean rebuild. Above-step reflection comes from wave-number mismatch, including a downward step; R=((k1-k2)/(k1+k2))^2 for the ideal step.

source_of_truth: beat_sheet.json — edit the sheet, not this file

beats.label: This is Liam, in for Bear. A classical particle rolling downhill never bounces backward. A quantum w

beats.prompt: Classically, lower potential means greater kinetic energy. The particle speeds up and continues right. There is no turning point and no reason to reverse.
beats.label: Classically, lower potential means greater kinetic energy. The particle speeds up and continues righ

beats.prompt: So what can possibly send a quantum wave back?
beats.label: So what can possibly send a quantum wave back?

beats.prompt: On the left, the wave number is k one. On the lower-potential right, the same total energy gives more kinetic energy and a larger wave number k two, so the wave
beats.label: On the left, the wave number is k one. On the lower-potential right, the same total energy gives mor

beats.prompt: The wave function and its derivative must join continuously at the boundary. One incoming wave alone cannot match a transmitted wave with a different wavelength
beats.label: The wave function and its derivative must join continuously at the boundary. One incoming wave alone

beats.prompt: The solution adds a reflected wave on the left. Incoming plus reflected amplitudes supply exactly the boundary value and slope needed by the transmitted wave.
beats.label: The solution adds a reflected wave on the left. Incoming plus reflected amplitudes supply exactly th

beats.prompt: For an abrupt step, the reflected amplitude is k one minus k two over k one plus k two. The reflected probability is its magnitude squared.
beats.label: For an abrupt step, the reflected amplitude is k one minus k two over k one plus k two. The reflecte

beats.prompt: If the two wave numbers nearly match, reflection is tiny. Increase the mismatch, and the reflected fraction grows. The boundary behaves like an impedance change
beats.label: If the two wave numbers nearly match, reflection is tiny. Increase the mismatch, and the reflected f

beats.prompt: Nothing is classically forbidden here. Both k values are real, and the transmitted wave propagates. Reflection comes from abrupt matching, not from insufficient
beats.label: Nothing is classically forbidden here. Both k values are real, and the transmitted wave propagates.

beats.prompt: Smooth the same potential change over many wavelengths and the wave adapts gradually, so reflection becomes much smaller. Abruptness is the essential ingredient
beats.label: Smooth the same potential change over many wavelengths and the wave adapts gradually, so reflection

beats.prompt: This is why a downhill step can reflect while a gently descending ramp scarcely does. Quantum particles inherit the boundary behavior of waves.
beats.label: This is why a downhill step can reflect while a gently descending ramp scarcely does. Quantum partic

beats.prompt: Enough energy does not guarantee zero reflection. A sudden wavelength mismatch splits the wave, even across a potential drop.
beats.label: Enough energy does not guarantee zero reflection. A sudden wavelength mismatch splits the wave, even

The particle clears the cliff. The wave still notices the edge.
